UNIT 4 TOWARDS 4 POLITICAL ETHICS: MORAL PHILOSOPHY OR CULTURAL NORMS

Structure 4.0 Objectives 4.1 Introduction 4.2 Excerpts from Book २४ 4.2 Lessons from the War 4.4 Let Us Sum Up 4.5 Questions 4.6 Glossary 4.7 Suggested Readings References

4.0

In the previous units we discussed the hero of the Iliad and the twin aspects of war that bestows honour on the victor and horror on the victims. In this unit: We will discuss whether the Iliad is just a narration of events presenting the seamy side of human action of violence, anger, revenge and frenzy or OU if it has a moral philosophy to offer. ( This is best understood from Book २४ when King Priam meets Achilles the Greek warrior and slayer of Hector his (Priam s) son. ) We give excerpts from the last Book that details the meeting between Priam and Achilles . text:
4.0 In the previous units we discussed the hero of the Iliad and the twin aspects of war that bestows honour on the victor and horror on the victims. In this unit: We will discuss whet

her the Iliad is just a narration of events presenting the seamy side of human action of violence, anger, revenge and frenzy or OU if it has a moral philosophy to offer. ( This is best understood from Book २४ when King Priam meets Achilles the Greek warrior and slayer of Hector his (Priam s) son. ) We give excerpts from the last Book that details the meeting between Priam and Achilles . We look at the morals and political ethics depicted in the Iliad EOPLES 4.1 INTRODUCTION RSITY Prince Paris of Troy elopes with Helen, the wife of King Menealaus the younger brother of the great King Agamemnon. In order to regain their honour, the Greeks go to war with the Trojans, lead by Agamemnon, Menealaus and Achilles the great warrior. There is a falling out between Agamemnon and Achilles over a slave girl; hence, Achilles refuses to fight. The Greeks start losing the war Achilles friend Patroclus is killed by Hector. In retaliation Achilles kills Hector and drags his body all over the battle field. War is going on in the background. What does such a depiction say about war, the reasons for battle, and the outcomes? In the Iliad we realise, that the Trojan War is actually a case of bruised egos and honours The two nations republics go to war over the elopement of Helen and Paris. It is the ethos of bravery, the age of valour but was this valour to be utilised for regaining lost honour alone? Would such a motivation for battle leave a lot of collateral damage? The answer is yes, as we realise that half the actions of the Iliad are dictated by: The elopement of Helen with Paris The demand for the return and surrender of Chryser s slave girl as a part of the war booty The outcome of the battle again is clear as Hector the Trojan has a clear indication of his impending death in the battle, while Achilles and the Greeks emerge victorious. In the next section we shall look at some of the excerpts from Book २४ of the Iliad that examine the notion of the Trojan War 33

Homer: The Iliad

text:
4.2 EXCERPTS FROM BOOK 24

The passages italicised highlight the nobility of Priam and Achilles. BkXXIV:468-551 Priam moves Achilles heart With that, Hermes left for high Olympus, while Priam climbed down from his chariot, leaving Idaeus to handle the horses and mules. The old king went straight to the hut frequented by Zeus beloved Achilles. He found him there, with only the warrior Automedon and warlike Alcimus of all his friends They were busy attending to his meal. The table stood at his side but he had finished eating and drinking Great Priam slipped in unobserved, and reaching Achilles, clasped his knees, and kissed his hands, the fearful, man-killing hands that had slaughtered SO many of his sons. Achilles was astonished at the sight of godlike Priam, AS were his friends. They stared at each other, astounded, AS men do in the hall of a wealthy nobleman, when a stranger who has murdered a man in a moment of frenzy in his OWn country, seeking refuge abroad, bursts in On them . Priam was already entreating Achilles : Godlike Achilles, think of your own father, who is of my generation, and so is likewise On the sad threshold of old age. Perhaps his neighbours are troubling him, and there is no On९ to protect him from harm, 01 ward off ruin. But he at least CAn rejoice in the knowledge that you live, and each day brings the hope 0f seeing yOu return Jrom Troy LE$ ITY (Priam pleads for Hector s body) Workshop of Bernard Picart, 1७१० While 1 1 am victim of sad fate. Of the best of my SOnS, the best in all of Troy not one is left. Fifty sons I had, when yOu Achaeans landed, nineteen by the one wife, and the rest by other ladies of my court. Most of them have fallen in furious battle, and the defender of the city and its people, my prime recourse, Hector you have killed, AS he fought for his country [ come now to the ships to beg his corpse from you, bringing A princely ransom. Respect the gods, Achilles, and show mercy towards me, remembering your OWN father, for [ am more to be pitied than he, since have brought myself to do what no other man 0n earth would do, I have lifted to my lips the hand of the man who killed my S0nS. His words had moved Achilles to tears at the thought of his OWn father, Towards a Political Ethics: and taking the old man s hands he set him gently from him, while both Moral Philosophy or Cultural Norms were lost in memory Priam remembered man killing Hector and wept aloud, at Achilles feet, while Achilles wept for his father Peleus and for Patroclus OnCe more, and the sound of their lament filled the hut. But when Achilles was sated with weeping and the force of grief was spent, he rose instantly from his chair and raising the old king by his arm, he took pity On his grey beard and hair and spoke eloquently to him: You are indeed unfortunate, and your heart has endured much sorrow Surely, though, there is iron in your spirit, daring to come alone to OUY ships, and face the man who slew SO many of your noble sons? Come, sit here, and we will shut away our SOrrOWS, despite OUY grief, since there is but cold comfort in lament. The gods have spun the thread of fate for wretched mortals : we live in sorrow, while they are free from care. Two urns stand in Zeus ' palace containing the experiences he grants mortals, one holds blessings, the other ills Those who receive a mixture of the two meet with good and ill, but those whom the Thunderer only serves from the jar of ills becomes An outcast, driven over the face of the earth by despair wanderer honoured neither by gods nOr men. See how the gods showered glorious gifts on my father Peleus, from the moment of his birth, wealth and possessions beyond other men, kingship of the Myrmidons, and though but a mortal mAn, A goddess for a wife. Yet some god brought evil even to him, no crowd of princes, but An only son doomed to An untimely end. OU He receives nO care from me, since I sit here in the land of Troy far from my OWn country bringing harm to yOu and your children. And yOU, my aged lord, they say you OnCe were happy, renowned for yOur wealth and yOUr SOnS, in all the lands, from the isle of Lesbos,, where Macar reigned, through upper Phyrgia to the boundless Hellespont. But from the moment OPLES that the heavenly gods brought this wretched war upon you, all has turned to battle

text:
4.6 GLOSSARY

Thunderer Myrmidions

Zeus १ follower or subordinate of a powerful person, typically one who is unscrupulous Or carries out orders unquestioningly

Homer: The Iliad

Niobe

the prototype of the bereaved mother, weeping for the loss of her children. Leto Goddess of womanly motherhood House of Hades abode of the dead, the netherworld Nymphs in ancient Greek mythology female deities of nature, living in the mountains, forests, seas, and springs. The nymphs are believed to be daughters of Zeus Ilium Troy Dardanian the inhabitant of Troy Barrow 4 large mound of earth or stones placed over a burial site. Clad cover Danaans Achaean, Greek people whom Homer identifies as the besiegers of Troy Their capital was Mycenae. Innerness of or pertaining to the mind or spirit; mental; spiritual Empathy the power of entering into another s personality and imaginatively experiencing his feelings. Immanence Existing or remaining within; inherent Contingency The condition of being dependent on chance; uncertainty
